---
title: "Spatial patterns of COVID-19 mortality in U.S. counties"
subtitle: "Sole-authored spatial-epidemiology study: cluster detection and spatial regression on socioeconomic determinants"
description: "A sole-authored spatial analysis of COVID-19 mortality across 3,000+ U.S. counties using SaTScan cluster detection and spatial lag/error regression on socioeconomic determinants."
---

::: {.tags .project-tags}
[Spatial epidemiology]{.tag} [Cluster detection]{.tag} [Spatial regression]{.tag} [Health disparities]{.tag} [R]{.tag}
:::

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
**Preprint**, sole-authored. Wardrup RR, medRxiv (2024).
[doi:10.1101/2024.10.30.24316443](https://www.medrxiv.org/content/10.1101/2024.10.30.24316443v1).
Not yet peer-reviewed.
:::

## Problem

COVID-19 mortality was not spread evenly across the United States, and the
county-level differences didn't look random: neighboring counties tended to
resemble each other, and the burden lined up in places with structural
disadvantage. Two questions follow from that. Where does county-level mortality
actually cluster? And once you account for the fact that counties near each
other aren't independent, which socioeconomic factors are associated with the
pattern?

That second question is where ordinary regression gets you into trouble.
County-level data has strong spatial autocorrelation, and ignoring it biases
both the coefficients and their standard errors. So the analysis has to be built
to respect the spatial structure from the start.

## Data and approach

The unit of analysis is the U.S. county, over 3,000 of them. County-level
COVID-19 mortality is the outcome; the covariates are socioeconomic, including
racial composition, income inequality, and uninsured rates. The approach runs in
two stages: first locate *where* mortality clusters, then model the socioeconomic
associations with regression that accounts for the spatial dependence.

## Methods

- **Cluster detection.** SaTScan to identify counties with statistically
  significant mortality trends and locate the clusters geographically.
- **Spatial regression.** Spatial lag and spatial error models to estimate the
  socioeconomic associations while accounting for spatial autocorrelation, with
  a non-spatial (OLS) model as the baseline for comparison.
- **Diagnostics.** Checks for multicollinearity among the socioeconomic
  covariates and for residual spatial autocorrelation, with model fit compared
  across the lag, error, and OLS specifications.

## Findings

- Mortality clusters are real and spatially structured, with regions in the
  northwestern and eastern/northeastern U.S. showing a mix of increasing and
  decreasing trends rather than a single national gradient.
- The **spatial lag model fit best**, which is the methodological headline:
  spatial dependence carries real information, and a model that ignores it
  misrepresents the associations.
- The socioeconomic covariates were associated with the mortality-trend outcome
  in the spatial models. Because the design is ecological and the outcome is a
  county-level trend, the paper frames those associations as descriptive rather
  than causal.
